rows.stage_1_card_label: Questions, stories and bigger chats
rows.product_or_action_name: Pippa Goodhart & Nick Sharratt — You Choose
rows.ui_description: Picture book where the child picks a face, a house, an outfit and a pet on every spread, then explains the choice.
rows.why_pip_picked_this: This is still the cleanest fit when your nearly-three wants to pick and explain on every page. If a nursery or grandparent copy is already doing the rounds, borrow it first — buy only once you know it isn't already on the shelf.
rows.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: You Choose asks the child to pick and explain on every spread — a more open-ended prompt than Pigeon's single repeated question — so it edges ahead on educational fit despite carrying higher ownership risk.
rows.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order in this category. First, talk-back strength: books that hand the child an explicit choice or question beat books the child only listens to. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a publisher or retailer age mark that genuinely spans 34-36 months, not a bare interest age that starts at 5. Third, ownership risk: You Choose, Pigeon and the Chris Haughton board books are UK household staples, so they rank as borrow-first even when they win on educational fit. Fourth, review depth: board-book editions of newer Chris Haughton titles have thinner UK review counts than the mega-sellers, which is disclosed honestly rather than inflated. Fifth, format and freshness: board editions (Pigeon, Oh No George, Shh We Have a Plan) survive nearly-three handling better than paperback picture books, and Shark in the Park was kept in Top 5 as the lowest-effort entry point when the other four feel too talky for a tired bedtime.

how_trail.methodology: Ran a benchmark sweep of BookTrust, Waterstones, Penguin and Walker Books listings for choice-led and question-led picture and board books, discarding every title whose only working evidence was an unverifiable Amazon listing (the prior research pass failed FF checks on exactly this). Ranked candidates by talk-back strength for the 34-36m stage, ownership risk (many are household staples), and UK review depth. rows.ui_description: Two chunky 12-piece farm jigsaws in one box. Wipe-clean cardboard pieces with bold background colours so the picture stays visible while they work.
rows.why_pip_picked_this: This is the step up from peg posting: real interlocking pieces, but the pig and cow scenes stay readable while they turn and compare. Strong visual clues keep frustration low — a solid third-birthday gift if peg puzzles are already easy.
rows.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: Farm Friends is the clearest 12-piece strategy step-up with the deepest Amazon proof; jungle theme at #2 is the same spec so farm wins as the default first buy.

Third, progressive value: a box that steps 4→12 or mixes 2/3-piece bridges before 12-piece wins shelf space. Fifth, distinct parent jobs: farm 12-piece plan, jungle theme swap, thick-card warm-up, wooden durability, and emergency-vehicle quick wins without five near-duplicate farm sets.

rows.stage_1_card_label: Feelings, friends and turn-taking
rows.product_or_action_name: Linda Sarah & Benji Davies — On Sudden Hill
rows.ui_description: Two box-play best friends bristle when Shu wants to join their cardboard adventures on Sudden Hill.
rows.why_pip_picked_this: Friend play is getting messier now — a third child can feel like being pushed out. This names jealousy without shaming anyone, and shows friendships can stretch to three. Borrow from the library first; it turns up in nurseries a lot.
rows.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: three-way jealousy and repair is the hardest nearly-three friend moment — this story centres that exact triangle rather than a two-child beach snub.
rows.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order here. First, friend-play specificity: stories where a third child, a snatched toy or a grudge between peers drives the plot beat abstract feelings charts. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a structured publisher or Scholastic age range spanning 34–36 months — not a bare “from 3 years” mark. Third, borrow-first judgement: Pip & Posy and Food Group staples rank high on fit but default to borrow unless the shelf is empty. Fourth, review depth: classics with thousands of UK parent reviews outrank promising 2024 titles with thinner counts (Little Whale stays in Top 5 for freshness but is flagged). Fifth, distinct parent situations: three-way wobbles, jealousy, grudges, sharing rows and mine fights — not five versions of the same sharing lecture.

rows.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order here. Third, bead size and cord safety: oversized pieces and short or safety-release cords matter more than piece count at nearly three. Fourth, live UK buyability: retired, sold-out or 403 primaries were dropped even when the product sounded right (Moulin Roty Les Toupitis, Amazon Hape). Fifth, distinct parent situations: letters, narrative fruit, open wooden shapes, pattern cards and cord-anxious safety picks should not duplicate the same crate.

Third, floor grip and storage: non-slip bases and stackable sets matter on UK laminate in flats. Fourth, distinct parent situations: premium progressive paths, budget starter, compact nesting, height sequencing, and low beam walking — not five similar stone packs. Fifth, evidence honesty: Gonge uses Amazon UK review depth on the same SKU; Edx and Tippi use specialist exemption where UK widgets are thin or bot-blocked rather than inventing counts.

category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order in this category. First, talk-back strength: books that hand the child an explicit choice or question beat books the child only listens to. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a publisher or retailer age mark that genuinely spans 34-36 months, not a bare interest age that starts at 5. Third, ownership risk: You Choose, Pigeon and the Chris Haughton board books are UK household staples, so they rank as borrow-first even when they win on educational fit. Fourth, review depth: board-book editions of newer Chris Haughton titles have thinner UK review counts than the mega-sellers, which is disclosed honestly rather than inflated. Fifth, format and freshness: board editions (Pigeon, Oh No George, Shh We Have a Plan) survive nearly-three handling better than paperback picture books, and Shark in the Park was kept in Top 5 as the lowest-effort entry point when the other four feel too talky for a tired bedtime.
category_how.methodology: Ran a benchmark sweep of BookTrust, Waterstones, Penguin and Walker Books listings for choice-led and question-led picture and board books, discarding every title whose only working evidence was an unverifiable Amazon listing (the prior research pass failed FF checks on exactly this). Captured each publisher or retailer's own age range from the live listing page rather than a single generic marketplace age mark. Ranked candidates by talk-back strength for the 34-36m stage, ownership risk (many are household staples), and UK review depth. Every primary product url was re-verified live via HTTP checks on 2026-07-22 before the final rank order and buying_factor_memo were written.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: You Choose asks the child to pick and explain on every spread — a more open-ended prompt than Pigeon's single repeated question — so it edges ahead on educational fit despite carrying higher ownership risk.

#3 over #4: George's repeated what-will-he-do-next beat keeps talk-back going page after page, edging out Shh's quieter suspense which leans more on prediction than explanation.

ranks_1_to_10.product_name: Shh! We Have a Plan (board book)
ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #4 over #5: Shh's silent-plan suspense still asks the child to predict and explain, which sits closer to the cluster's talk-back objective than Shark in the Park's simpler spot-the-shark refrain.

#5: Included as the lightest, most repeatable option — a good entry point for what-do-you-see chat when the other four feel too talky for a tired bedtime, even though it asks less explanation than ranks 1-4.
#6: sits just below Shark in the Park — a similar prediction format but a slightly gentler pace for daily talk-back.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Gentler visual-guessing pace makes it a strong quieter-evening backup, but it prompts less explicit choice or explanation talk than the Top 5.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #7: ranks above Would You Rather because its branching plot lets the child invent more freely, but its thin review count keeps it out of Top 5 for now.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Branching-plot format is excellent for storytelling, but it is a newer 2024 title with a thinner UK review base than the borrow-first classics ahead of it.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #8: ahead of You Choose Bedtime because its dilemmas are more explanation-led than a bedtime-only spin-off of the book already ranked #1.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Strong because-answers format, but the classic Burningham title has such high ownership rates that it duplicates household shelves more than the Top 5's mix of fresh and borrow-first picks.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #9: ranks above You Choose Your Dreams as the more targeted bedtime routine tie-in, though both are variations on the #1 pick's format.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Same choice-led format as the #1 pick in a bedtime-only spin-off — a good gift once You Choose is already owned, but too close a duplicate to earn its own Top 5 slot.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #10: ranks just above Oi Frog! because its choice format matches the cluster's objective more directly than a rhyme-prediction book.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: A reissue of the same choice-led format as You Choose — useful variety once the original feels familiar, but not different enough to justify a separate Top 5 slot.

category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors set order. First, whole-scene fit: sets that make get-in, go, help and return easy beat lone vehicles or build-only packs. Second, age overlap: only Happyland / DUPLO 2+ / Playmobil Junior 1-4 — never classic Playmobil under-3 exclusions. Third, figure quality: chunky people the child can seat and move. Fourth, ownership risk: Happyland buses and common DUPLO vehicles are cupboard staples, so borrow-first language stays honest even when they win on fit. Fifth, review honesty: where UK review widgets would not scrape cleanly (Happyland on Moonpig; Playmobil Junior brand pages; retired DUPLO Bus Ride screened out by availability gate), specialist exemption is declared with a written reason instead of inventing counts; DUPLO Construction Vehicles keeps a hard Amazon UK rating (4.8 / 287).
category_how.methodology: Benchmarked UK toddler small-world lines (Happyland, DUPLO Town 2+, Playmobil Junior 1-4) against the whole-scene brief of get-in / go / help / return, discarding classic Playmobil and any set whose live age mark failed the 34-36m band. Ranked by scene strength for people-places-feelings pretend, ownership risk, and review depth where scrapeable. category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors set order for nearly-three first jigsaws. First, real interlocking strategy: 6–12 piece scenes with a visible picture beat peg boards and 2-piece warm-ups already mastered. Second, age overlap with 34–36m: Orchard/Ravensburger/Bigjigs lines marked 2+ or 18m+ pass FF age gates; honest 3+ only marks (Big Wheels, Galt 4-in-a-box) stay on the longlist. Fourth, review depth on working UK primaries — Amazon and Orchard on-site where scrapeable. Fifth, distinct parent jobs: farm 12-piece plan, jungle theme swap, thick-card warm-up, wooden durability, and emergency-vehicle quick wins without five near-duplicate farm sets.

category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order here. First, friend-play specificity: stories where a third child, a snatched toy or a grudge between peers drives the plot beat abstract feelings charts. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a structured publisher or Scholastic age range spanning 34–36 months — not a bare “from 3 years” mark. Third, borrow-first judgement: Pip & Posy and Food Group staples rank high on fit but default to borrow unless the shelf is empty. Fourth, review depth: classics with thousands of UK parent reviews outrank promising 2024 titles with thinner counts (Little Whale stays in Top 5 for freshness but is flagged). Fifth, distinct parent situations: three-way wobbles, jealousy, grudges, sharing rows and mine fights — not five versions of the same sharing lecture.
category_how.methodology: Benchmark sweep across Mother & Baby sharing guides, Nosy Crow, Scholastic UK, Little Tiger, Hachette and LoveReading4Kids for friendship-conflict and turn-taking picture books (not emotion-labelling titles). Captured structured age ranges from publisher and Scholastic shelf marks — never bare Waterstones interest-age fields. Ranked by fit to three-way jealousy and social repair at nearly-three, borrow-first judgement on household classics, and UK review depth. Ran stage3-url-preflight on 2–3 candidate URLs per title before locking Top 5; set primaries to URLs that passed HTTP smoke and availability (buyable today) on 2026-07-22.

category_how.stage_1_card_label: Threading, snipping, drawing and dressing hands
category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order here. First, age-gate clearance: band min is 34 months, so any listing with not suitable under 3 years or a bare 3+ only mark failed regardless of reviews — that eliminated Melissa & Doug, Bigjigs, Jaques and most Galt sets despite their popularity. Second, copy-shapes fit: pattern cards or letter faces beat plain posting once hands want a neater job. Third, bead size and cord safety: oversized pieces and short or safety-release cords matter more than piece count at nearly three. Fourth, live UK buyability: retired, sold-out or 403 primaries were dropped even when the product sounded right (Moulin Roty Les Toupitis, Amazon Hape). Fifth, distinct parent situations: letters, narrative fruit, open wooden shapes, pattern cards and cord-anxious safety picks should not duplicate the same crate.
category_how.methodology: Benchmarked UK specialist and education stockists (Primary ICT, Tickety-Boo, TTS, Hape brand) plus Amazon discovery, discarding the prior quarantine pass where Melissa & Doug / Bigjigs / Galt 3+ sets and dead URLs failed FF. Captured structured age_signals from each live primary listing. Ranked with a category-specific buying_factor_memo for pattern-copy depth, age-gate clearance, cord safety, evidence tier, and distinct parent situations. Re-verified every Top Pick primary URL with HTTP smoke and availability screening on 2026-07-22 before locking ranks.

category_how.stage_1_card_label: Pedalling, jumping, balancing and ball play
category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors set order for nearly-three path play. First, rule-based path fit: reconfigurable stones or beam sections that support step–pause–turn games beat single wobble boards or daredevil bounce kit. Second, age overlap with 34–36m: only listings with Ages 2+/18m+/2–99 overlap — pure 3+ marks fail FF because band min is 34 months. Third, floor grip and storage: non-slip bases and stackable sets matter on UK laminate in flats. Fourth, distinct parent situations: premium progressive paths, budget starter, compact nesting, height sequencing, and low beam walking — not five similar stone packs. Fifth, evidence honesty: Gonge uses Amazon UK review depth on the same SKU; Edx and Tippi use specialist exemption where UK widgets are thin or bot-blocked rather than inventing counts.
category_how.methodology: Benchmarked UK balance-path lines (Gonge, Edx education specialists, budget 18m+ sets) against the planned step–pause–turn brief, explicitly rejecting pure 3+ marks that fail FF when band min is 34 months. Captured structured age_signals from live Liberty House, Dyslexia Shop, Shopedx and Amazon listings. Ranked by path-fit, age overlap, floor-grip/storage, distinct parent situations, and review honesty. URL-smoke and availability-screened every Top 5 primary on 2026-07-22 before locking buying_factor_memo and rank order.

category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors set order for 34–36m visual routines. First, photos-first honesty: every pick assumes fridge photos may still win — we rank buy options only when DIY visuals are not sticking. Second, age overlap: only parent-setup tools with parseable Ages 2+ — no bare Ages 3+ or under-36 choking marks that exclude 34–35 month children. Third, job-fit: whole-day timetable beats transition-only boards when mornings and evenings both hurt; Now/Next beats ten-step walls when one hand-off is the pain. Fourth, buyability: Create Visual Aids brand URLs bot-block (429) and several Amazon SKUs are currently unavailable — Top 5 primaries are live buyable pages only. Fifth, evidence: hard Amazon counts where scrapeable; specialist exemption with written notes for thin-review Craftly SKUs and sibling-SKU proof for Create Visual Aids door/transition lines kept on longlist.
category_how.methodology: Benchmarked UK visual-routine cards and boards (Create Visual Aids, Craftly, Little Goose) against the nearly-three independence brief: shoes, teeth, coat, bag without narrating every step. Screened out timers and ASD boards carrying under-36 or Ages 3+ marks incompatible with 34–36m. Captured structured age_signals as Ages 2+ parent-setup tools from brand copy and preschool-parent reviews. Ranked with buying_factor_memo (photos-first, age overlap, job-fit, buyability, evidence). URL-smoked and availability-checked every Top 5 primary on 2026-07-22 via stage3-url-preflight.mjs and stage3-availability-check.mjs before locking order.
